name: Invariants & Parity — What Never Changes
whatIsIt: An INVARIANT is a quantity that stays the SAME no matter what moves you make. The simplest invariant is PARITY — whether a number is even or odd. If every allowed move keeps a total even, you can never reach an odd total. Spotting an invariant lets you prove a puzzle is IMPOSSIBLE without trying every case — a favourite Olympiad move.
whyNeeded: Many contest problems ask 'can you reach this state?' Testing endlessly never proves 'no'. Finding an invariant that the goal breaks proves impossibility in one line.
realLifeUses: Proving a coin-flip puzzle can never reach all-heads
Showing a knight can't return in an odd number of moves
Checking a chessboard tiling is impossible after removing two squares
Detecting errors with parity check bits in computers

vocabulary.meaning: A quantity unchanged by every allowed move.
Whether a number is even or odd.
Using an invariant the target breaks to show it can't be reached.

story.title: The Coin Flip
story.text: Three coins show Heads-Heads-Tails. Aditya may flip exactly TWO coins at a time. 'Can I make all three Heads?' Didi counts Tails: start with 1 (odd). Flipping two coins changes the Tails-count by −2, 0, or +2 — always by an EVEN amount, so the parity of the Tails-count never changes. All-Heads needs 0 Tails (even), but he started ODD. 'Impossible!' she says. Aditya grins: 'The parity of Tails is the invariant.'
story.extractedProblem: Flipping two coins at a time, can HHT become HHH?
story.answerInStory: No — the number of Tails starts odd (1) and each move changes it by an even amount, so it can never reach 0 (even).

lines.label: Tails count stays ODD: 1 → 3 → 1 …; can never hit 0

visual.caption: An invariant fences off the impossible: the Tails-count keeps its parity, so an odd start can never reach an even goal like 0.

teachingGallery.title: Parity is preserved
teachingGallery.note: Even ± even stays even; odd stays odd.

lines.label: Even sum stays even under ±2 moves
Odd stays odd — never reaches an even target

standardMethod.summary: Look for a quantity (often a parity, sum, or colouring) that every allowed move leaves unchanged. Compute it at the start and at the goal. If they differ, the goal is impossible.
steps: Find a quantity each move leaves unchanged (an invariant) — parity is the first to try.
Compute the invariant at the start and at the target.
If they don't match, reaching the target is impossible.

workedExamples.problem: Coins show HHT (1 Tail). Flipping two at a time, can you reach HHH (0 Tails)?
steps: Flipping two changes Tails by −2, 0, or +2 (parity fixed).

workedExamples.problem: Is the parity of 7 unchanged after adding 4?

commonMistakes.mistake: Claiming a quantity is invariant without checking every move keeps it.
commonMistakes.fix: Verify EACH allowed move leaves the quantity unchanged before using it as an invariant.

commonMistakes.mistake: Thinking adding any number keeps parity.
commonMistakes.fix: Adding an EVEN number keeps parity; adding an ODD number flips it.

Start odd, each move keeps parity. Can you reach an even total?

challenge.q: HHT → HHH by flipping two at a time is…
Removing two opposite corners of a chessboard leaves 30 black + 32 white. A domino tiling is…

questions.q: To prove a state is unreachable, find a quantity that is…

options.label: Invariant and differs at the goal

questions.answer: Invariant and differs at the goal

questions.q: Adding an even number to a number…
Start with 1 Tail; moves change Tails by even amounts. Reach 0 Tails?

teachBackPrompt: With three coins (HHT), show a grown-up the two-coin-flip rule and explain why all-Heads is impossible: the number of Tails starts odd and every move changes it by an even amount, so it can never become 0. Name the invariant (parity of Tails).

revisionCard.summary: Invariant = a quantity every move leaves unchanged; parity (even/odd) is the go-to one. If the start and goal have different invariant values, the goal is impossible. Even shift keeps parity, odd shift flips it. Check every move truly preserves it.

rememberIt.hook: Find what never changes — if the goal breaks it, it's impossible
rememberIt.unpack: Parity is the first invariant to try. Even shift keeps parity; odd shift flips it.

studentQuestions.q: Besides parity, what else can be an invariant?
studentQuestions.a: A colouring (like a chessboard), a sum mod some number, or a product's sign. Anything every move leaves unchanged can be an invariant.

studentQuestions.q: Can an invariant prove something IS possible?
studentQuestions.a: No — a matching invariant only fails to rule it out. Invariants prove impossibility; you still need a construction to prove possibility.
